speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 3, interviewing William McKinney. Mr. McKinney, in the previous tape, you were speaking of being in Paris and other French cities. What direction did you need to-- has gone from Paris? How long were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: After we left Paris, we went on to Liege, Belgium. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get-- how did you travel there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Military trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Military convoys, were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that of a trip to Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the exact mileage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a day? How long did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was less than a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well in Belgium, Liege fell first. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fell to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the American troops, the United States First Army. The United States First Army was the lead army under General Hodges and General Omar Bradley. And Liege fell. Then a little town nestled in between [NON-ENGLISH] and Verviers. Verviers was the next. See we were on the move. And a lot of these little towns and things that we'd pass through, I don't remember the names of them all. Then, after Liege, Verviers. Then we went to a town called Raeren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raeren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it still-- was it still--

speaker: interviewee
text: R-E-A-D-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it set in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was part-- it was on the Belgian-German border. And it was just outside of Aachen, Germany. Aachen, Germany was the first German town that decided to fall. And 12 hours after Aachen fell-- our outfit was in Aachen. Then from Aachen, we went and crossed the Rhine. When we crossed the Rhine, [NON-ENGLISH], Dusseldorf, Duren, Baden-Baden, [INAUDIBLE]. All of these German towns nothing but shells of buildings. In the wake of that First Army. And from Raeren they had these big guns. 75 millimeters, 105 millimeters, 155 millimeters, 175 millimeters, 205 millimeters. The 205 would shoot 23 miles accurately on a clear day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had those guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are American guns. And I was right up under those guns. And where we fired them, open craters in the earth as wide as this room when they fired those guns. And they were shelling across the Rhine.

words.text: open craters in the earth as

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you to-- to-- to hear this shelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was nerve-racking. It was really nerve-racking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, at any time in your experience, have direct contact with the German soldier? Did you have any of that experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the Germans surrendered, they had set out points where they had compounds for these prisoners, German prisoners of war. And our outfit marched them back to these compounds. And [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] and during the Battle of the Bulge, that was when Hitler amassed this big army and pushed us back to the channel. They pushed the ridge and the point of the United States First Army. And until this day, the strategy on the American part. They let them push in. Then Patton came out from right flank. The Ninth Army came down on the left flank. And they had these Germans surrounded in a pocket, and they came out of there by the thousands. See, Hitler had amassed this big army under General von Rundstedt. And then, until this day, I believe that it was military strategy on the high command of the United States Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you, at any time when you were in Germany, reached a point of where there were any concentration camps or extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, we reached Weimar, Germany. That's where-- that's the town that I was in when the war ended, May 8th. They had this camp, Buchenwald. [COUGHS]. They had this camp, Buchenwald. And there were thousands of Jewish refugees in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe, in greater detail, when you entered the camp? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I saw-- it was in deplorable condition, this camp. I saw the Jewish refugees dressed in blue and gray stripe-like uniforms. They looked like pajamas.

speaker: interviewer
text: They looked like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pajamas. And then they had other prisoners of war mixed in with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does people--

speaker: interviewee
text: British and Americans that had been captured.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the physical condition of-- of those prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, their eyes were sunken in, and their faces were sunken in. They started to look like skeletons with just skin over them. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There were two Jewish boys--

speaker: interviewer
text: I got to go to the restroom. Where's the restroom? Mr. McKinney, before you begin to speak of the two Jewish boys, let's go back to the camp itself. Can you tell me what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't in no camps. We were on the battlefields.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the-- in the Buchenwald itself, what else did you see inside of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw lamp shades made from human skin. And then I saw molds of the different parts of the airplanes-- every nut, bolt and screw and wire. And the [NON-ENGLISH], a fighter plane. The [INAUDIBLE] 118, the [INAUDIBLE] 119.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Weimar, just outside of Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go again to the Buchenwald itself. Have you seen dead bodies in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They'd have one dead, two alive, two or three dead bodies laying there. But see, I didn't bother with any of those bodies. Because we gave them-- the alive ones, K ration would kill them. Then the medical came. They said, we shouldn't give no K rations. They hadn't had anything salty to eat in such a long time, that it would kill them. And then they had to feed them intravenously when the medics came in, so they could get their strength, until they could get enough strength to eat solid food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anything else in the camp proper? Did you see any gas chambers? Or-- what else did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes the-- I saw the pits, where they'd march them out and shoot them and fall down in the pit.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where they'd march them out and shoot them and have them fall over in the pit.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the Germans doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see the Germans when they did it, no. But you-- what I saw was the aftermath. After we had conquered the German troops and run them out of there. This was the aftermath, what I've seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- how young were the people-- how old were the people, or how young were the people that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I don't know. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see children there who survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were speaking before of the two Jewish boys. Can you continue with that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were two little Jewish boys. As close as I can remember, their names were Paul and Jordan. One was about 4, one was about 6. And you know, when we-- we had a field there where we set up field kitchen. And the average soldier would eat what they want. Some had too much. They'd just throw it over in the garbage can, remaining. And these two little boys were digging out in the garbage can with their hands, eating this food. And I told them, no, no. So I communicated with them to the best of my ability, try to get them to understand. And we finally got to reach a point where we understood each other. Then I didn't-- [COUGHS]. I let the one eat. [DRINKS] I went and got a basket of food and let the one eat. And after he finished, and I got the mess-- refilled the mess through the line again, and refill the mess, and gave it to the other one. And they were clinging to me for two days. And then we had to move on. And I wanted to adopt them. And the authorities told me no, that they had set up orphanages for these children-- displaced persons, displaced children. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they were from? What country they were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, all I knew, I got out of them, was that they had made them stand and watch when they marched their mother and father into these furnaces to be burnt alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be burnt alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what they told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I got out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that how their parents were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how they became-- yes. That's how they became--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were burnt alive? Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Burnt alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say where? Was it at--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't say where. I just assumed that it was the furnaces at Buchenwald I don't know. See I didn't have time to go into a whole lot of particulars with the people that I encountered over there. I was over there doing a job. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was my job in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What kind of things you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same things that I did all the way through. I didn't have no specific tasks in Buchenwald. See they had Grave Registration Company, to handle dead bodies in there. And I was not in no Grave Registration. I thought I made myself explicitly clear that I was in the 978 Combat Services Supply. We handled ammunition, shells for the artillery and small arms, 50 caliber machine guns, rifle bullets, rifles, what have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, when you entered Buchenwald, how did those who were still alive respond when they saw you, American and other American soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were exuberant to see the Americans run those Germans out of there. And I understand they no longer had to be confronted with the atrocities that the Germans was committing against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what was it like for you to see all this slim skinny individuals in those striped uniforms? What was it like for you when you saw that? What went through your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the most deplorable thing that I had ever seen in my entire life. And I wouldn't want to see that again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until then, you were enlisted-- you were drafted in '43. Until 1943, have you heard-- when you were still, I guess, teenager-- what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you read--

speaker: interviewee
text: The news [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Then kind of things did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess the same things that everybody hear on the news. About all the turmoil and unrest over there in Europe. Because when I was over there, I thought after we defeated those Nazis. That was the war that would end all wars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you heard of-- in 1943, have you heard of the concentration camps by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have I heard anything about the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. In news media, the radio, and newspapers, magazines.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were informed what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There were Blitzkriegs and all that. Yes, I heard it on the news and read about it in the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they describe-- did they give some examples of-- of what was happening to-- to the Jewish people and other nationalities, of the brutalities in-- in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me. You were there in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they explained-- on the news here and read it in the newspaper, about the Blitzkriegs when the Nazis was invading all these countries in Europe, and how they were persecuting the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Buchenwald for how many days? For two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is how long you have been with the two boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't stay in Buchenwald. I saw Buchenwald, went through Buchenwald. I was in Weimar. Buchenwald was just outside of Weimar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a little bit more of those two boys? What did they look like, those two boys? Short, small, tall? How did they look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like little child. Maybe four or five and six, or four and six.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they-- What did they wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had some civilian clothing on. They didn't have the pajamas. They had regular little outfits for a boy of their age.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time did you spend with them during those two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whenever meal time would come around, I would catch them. They knew where we'd be serving food. And they would come. And I would get food for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have to move out of your base. How did they feel about you having-- needing to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say goodbye to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that moment, what did they do when you had to say goodbye? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they grabbed onto my legs when I had to say goodbye. I told them, I have to go. We're moving on.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wouldn't let go of your legs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They grabbed a hold of my legs. Each one was clinging to each one of my legs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that difficult for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just patted them on their shoulders and head. Told them, I have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after--

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one, I understood more. I said, I've been over here fighting a war. I don't have time to play nursemaid to you youngsters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the next stage for your unit? Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a town called Erfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Erfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Erfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. E-F-F-E-R-T. Erfurt, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well when the war ended, I was in there for about a month. About a month. When the war ended, I was still at Weimar. See, the war ended May 8th, and I was in Weimar, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that day when the declaration of end of war was made?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said May 8th.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, when you heard that the war is over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I felt very exhilarated that the war had ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to Erfurt, as I said. Then our outfit was transferred over to the occupation forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: What occupation force?

speaker: interviewee
text: The occupation army forces. We had occupied Germany. We had to set up, assist in the setting up the American military zone. They had Americans, the French, the Russians. And-- they had four zones. The Americans, French, Russians, and the British. Four different zones. And they'd leave troops there in these occupied zones to make sure there was no more uprisings. So I was attached to the occupation forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it started at Erfurt. Then they moved us to Hirschfeld, Germany. So like I said, I had seen much. Autobahn, even traveled the Autobahn. That's where they got the idea of turnpikes here in the United States, from Autobahn, built with slave labor. I traveled it [INAUDIBLE]. And there was-- see it's very difficult to remember in minute detail. It would take quite some time for me to explain everything that I had participated in, the towns and things that I had seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're coming to the end of this tape, and we'll continue on the next.
